title: Kink-kink correlations in nonlinear quenches across a quantum critical point
abstract: When a quantum system exhibiting a second order phase transition is quenched across the critical point in large but finite time, the dynamics are not adiabatic in the critical region and the Kibble-Zurek (KZ) mechanism provides a framework to determine local observables such as the mean defect density. However, to find higher-point functions, one has to go beyond the KZ paradigm asshown in recent works on one-dimensional transverse field Ising model (TFIM) following a linear quench. It has been found that (i) besides the KZ scale, the quench dynamics depend on another length scale that arises due to the finite phase difference between the low energy modes, and (ii) contrary to the expectations based on the KZ mechanism, in general, the correlation functions do not decay exponentially with distance. Motivated by these results for the linear quench, we are interested in understanding if these properties are universal, and consider the 1D TFIM when the transverse field varies algebraically in the vicinity of the critical field. We focus on the equal-time,longitudinal kink-kink correlation function at the end of the quench from the paramagnetic to the ferromagnetic phase, and find that (i) the correlator depends only on the KZ length for superlinear quenches, otherwise an additional dephasing length is required to describe it, and (ii) the dephased correlator decays as a compressed exponential with an exponent that changes continuously with the quench exponent. Our results are obtained using an adiabatic perturbation theory, analytical arguments and exact numerical integration of the relevant equations.
summary: This paper explores how nonlinear quench protocols affect the spatial correlations of defects in the 1D Transverse Field Ising Model. It identifies that a new dephasing length scale is necessary to describe correlations in sublinear and linear quenches, and shows that the decay behavior follows a compressed exponential pattern.
main_problem: Investigating the universality of kink-kink correlation functions in quantum systems undergoing nonlinear (algebraic) quenches across a second-order quantum critical point.
main_result: For superlinear quenches, the correlator depends only on the Kibble-Zurek length, whereas sublinear/linear quenches require an additional dephasing length; additionally, the dephased correlator decays as a compressed exponential with an exponent that varies with the quench exponent.
method: The authors use adiabatic perturbation theory, the method of stationary phase, and saddle-point approximations for analytical results, alongside exact numerical integration of the Bogoli-de Gennes equations.
model_or_system: The 1D Transverse Field Ising Model (TFIM) on a ring of N sites, where the transverse field g(t) varies algebraically near the critical point.
key_observables: Equal-time longitudinal kink-kink correlation function (dephased and off-diagonal components), excitation probability, and mean defect density.
important_parameters: Quench exponent (omega), quench time (tau), Kibble-Zurek length (xi-hat), and dephasing length (l).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be in the slow-quench regime, and the initial state is the ground state of the initial Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the nonlinear quench protocol and the resulting kink-kink correlator decay; Figure 3 compares excitation probability numerical and analytical results; Figure 8 demonstrates data collapse using different length scales; Figure 10 shows the effect of waiting time on dephasing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the nonlinear quench protocol, applies the Jordan-Wigner and Bogoliubov transformations, uses adiabatic perturbation theory and stationary phase approximations to derive scaling laws, provides numerical verification, and discusses the implications for dephasing and optimization.
why_interesting: It provides new insights into the non-equilibrium dynamics and universality of correlation functions beyond the standard Kibble-Zurek mechanism, which is highly relevant for understanding many-body dynamics and state preparation in quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
